skill.name: bigquery
skill.description: Guide for using the BigQuery bq CLI to inspect schemas, count rows, list tables, and run cautious metadata queries in specific analytics projects.
skill.summary: Use BigQuery CLI commands for cautious schema inspection, table discovery, row counts, and dbt validation.
skill.author: BfdCampos

security_audit.summary: Manual review found that the static Ruby backtick, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance alerts are mostly false positives caused by Markdown code examples and prose. The confirmed risk is contextual: the skill gives target-specific BigQuery project, dataset, PII, and export guidance that should not be published broadly without sanitization and authorization controls.

high_findings.title: Target-Specific BigQuery and PII Project Guidance
high_findings.description: The skill names specific Monzo BigQuery projects and datasets, including a PII project and raw event data. This is not malware, but it can assist reconnaissance or misuse by anyone with access to the referenced environment.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The named projects and restricted data categories are explicit in the skill text. The risk is contextual because access still depends on existing BigQuery credentials.
high_findings.remediation: Remove organization-specific project, dataset, and table names before marketplace publication. Replace them with generic placeholders and require explicit authorization for private data sources.

medium_findings.title: External BigQuery Commands Can Access Warehouse Data
medium_findings.description: The many bq command examples are documentation rather than executable code, so the static Ruby backtick findings are false positives. The commands still guide an agent to query BigQuery and should require user authorization, scoped credentials, and cost controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly examples inside Markdown fences, not code that runs automatically. They are still operational instructions for querying an external data system.
medium_findings.remediation: Publish with a warning that commands must be reviewed before execution. Prefer dry runs, metadata-only queries, explicit project IDs, and least-privilege credentials.

medium_findings.title: Local Export Examples Need Data Handling Guardrails
medium_findings.description: The skill includes examples that write query output to local files, including /tmp/columns.txt and output.csv. The shown /tmp example exports metadata only, but similar patterns can persist sensitive results if adapted carelessly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are explicit and the examples are benign metadata in one section. The broader export pattern could become sensitive when used with actual table data.
medium_findings.remediation: Add instructions to avoid exporting sensitive data, use approved storage locations, and remove local files after use.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Crypto and Reconnaissance Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptographic algorithm and system reconnaissance alerts do not correspond to cryptographic code, host inspection, or malware behavior in the Markdown file. No evidence found of hashing, credential harvesting, or system inventory commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found Markdown prose and SQL examples at these locations, not weak crypto APIs or system reconnaissance commands. The static labels appear to be keyword collisions.
low_findings.remediation: No security fix is needed for these specific static alerts. Keep the contextual BigQuery data-access risks documented.

dangerous_patterns.title: External BigQuery Commands Can Access Warehouse Data
dangerous_patterns.description: The many bq command examples are documentation rather than executable code, so the static Ruby backtick findings are false positives. The commands still guide an agent to query BigQuery and should require user authorization, scoped credentials, and cost controls.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly examples inside Markdown fences, not code that runs automatically. They are still operational instructions for querying an external data system.
dangerous_patterns.remediation: Publish with a warning that commands must be reviewed before execution. Prefer dry runs, metadata-only queries, explicit project IDs, and least-privilege credentials.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Export Examples Need Data Handling Guardrails
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes examples that write query output to local files, including /tmp/columns.txt and output.csv. The shown /tmp example exports metadata only, but similar patterns can persist sensitive results if adapted carelessly.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are explicit and the examples are benign metadata in one section. The broader export pattern could become sensitive when used with actual table data.
dangerous_patterns.remediation: Add instructions to avoid exporting sensitive data, use approved storage locations, and remove local files after use.

content.value_statement: BigQuery exploration can expose sensitive data or create expensive queries when agents guess commands. This skill provides repeatable bq CLI patterns for metadata-first inspection, row counts, table discovery, and cautious validation.
actual_capabilities: Shows bq CLI patterns for schema inspection with INFORMATION_SCHEMA.
Provides row count and table listing queries that avoid reading table contents.
Explains cautious sampling rules for non-sensitive BigQuery tables.
Includes output formatting options for CSV, JSON, and quiet command output.
Supports dbt workflows by checking source schemas and table existence.
limitations: It is tailored to specific Monzo project names and should be generalized before public use.
It does not authenticate users or manage BigQuery permissions.
It does not validate query cost beyond recommending dry runs.
It cannot determine whether a table contains sensitive data without user context.

use_cases.title: Validate Source Tables
use_cases.description: Check that BigQuery source tables exist and expose the expected columns before running dbt models.

use_cases.title: Explore Warehouse Metadata
use_cases.description: List tables, inspect column types, and count rows without reading sensitive business records.

use_cases.title: Debug Query Failures
use_cases.description: Use Standard SQL bq commands to diagnose missing tables, permission errors, and schema mismatches.

prompt_templates.title: Check a Table Schema
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the BigQuery skill to inspect the schema for PROJECT.DATASET.TABLE with INFORMATION_SCHEMA only. Show the bq command and explain what it returns.

prompt_templates.title: Find Matching Tables
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the BigQuery skill to find tables in DATASET that match SEARCH_TERM. Keep the query metadata-only and use Standard SQL.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a dbt Source Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the BigQuery skill to build a short validation plan before running dbt. Include schema checks, row counts, and permission checks for the source tables.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Risky Query Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this proposed bq query plan for sensitive data, cost, and export risks. Suggest safer metadata-first alternatives and dry-run checks.

output_examples.input: Inspect a table before building a dbt model.
output: A metadata-only schema check using INFORMATION_SCHEMA.
A row count query to confirm table size.
A reminder to avoid sampling sensitive columns without explicit approval.
A table discovery query for the target dataset.
A permissions checklist for access denied errors.
A Standard SQL syntax reminder for fully qualified table names.

output_examples.input: Export a list of column names for review.
output: A metadata export approach that avoids table contents.
A note to use approved local file handling.
A warning not to export PII or customer financial records.

best_practices: Use INFORMATION_SCHEMA before reading table contents.
Use explicit project IDs and fully qualified table names.
Run dry-run checks before expensive or broad queries.
anti_patterns: Do not publish organization-specific project names in a public skill.
Do not sample PII, staff, authentication, or customer financial tables without approval.
Do not export query results to local files unless the data classification allows it.

faq.question: Does this skill run BigQuery commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides command patterns and guidance that an agent or user may choose to run.

faq.question: Why is this skill rated high risk?
faq.answer: It includes target-specific BigQuery project and PII guidance that should be sanitized before public marketplace use.

faq.question: Can it inspect schemas without reading sensitive rows?
faq.answer: Yes. It emphasizes INFORMATION_SCHEMA queries for columns, tables, metadata, and row counts.

faq.question: Does it handle authentication?
faq.answer: No. BigQuery authentication and permissions must already be configured outside the skill.

faq.question: Can it help with dbt work?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide source table checks, schema validation, and debugging before dbt runs.

faq.question: What should be changed before publication?
faq.answer: Remove organization-specific project names, sensitive table examples, and any guidance tied to private data environments.
